title: Emergency Line in Budget
fullRewardHint: Answer all {{total}} choices correctly to earn the full reward.
reflectionPrompts: How can emergency funds provide peace of mind in uncertain times?
What balance between emergency savings and other financial goals works for your situation?

stages.prompt: Why include emergency buffer in your budget?

options.label: For fun spending
options.reflection: Emergency funds are specifically for unexpected expenses, not discretionary spending.

options.label: To handle surprises without debt
options.reflection: Exactly! Emergency buffers prevent you from going into debt when unexpected expenses arise.

options.label: To invest in high-risk opportunities
options.reflection: Emergency funds should be kept in safe, liquid accounts for quick access when needed.

options.label: To save for vacation money
options.reflection: Vacations are planned expenses and should be saved for separately from emergency funds.

stages.prompt: What percentage of your income should go to emergency fund?

options.label: 0% - I'll borrow when needed
options.reflection: Relying on borrowing creates debt and financial stress during emergencies.

options.label: 3-5% for basic protection
options.reflection: Good start! This provides some cushion for small unexpected expenses.

options.label: 20%+ for maximum security
options.reflection: While thorough, this might be excessive for most people's needs and lifestyle.

options.label: 10-15% for solid emergency fund
options.reflection: Perfect! This builds a robust emergency fund that can handle most unexpected situations.

stages.prompt: When should you tap into your emergency fund?

options.label: Medical emergencies or job loss
options.reflection: Correct! These are exactly the situations emergency funds are designed to cover.

options.label: Anytime I want extra money
options.reflection: Using emergency funds for non-emergencies defeats their purpose and leaves you vulnerable.

options.label: To upgrade my lifestyle items
options.reflection: Lifestyle upgrades should be planned for separately, not funded by emergency reserves.

options.label: To take advantage of investment opportunities
options.reflection: Investment opportunities require planned savings, not emergency fund money.

stages.prompt: How much should your emergency fund cover?

options.label: One month of expenses
options.reflection: This provides minimal protection and may not cover larger emergencies adequately.

options.label: Twelve months of expenses
options.reflection: While comprehensive, this might be overkill for most people and tie up too much money.

options.label: Three to six months of expenses
options.reflection: Ideal! This provides substantial protection against job loss or major medical expenses.

options.label: Enough to last my entire lifetime
options.reflection: This is impractical and would prevent you from investing and growing your wealth.

stages.prompt: Where should you keep your emergency fund?

options.label: In volatile stocks for higher returns
options.reflection: Stocks are too risky for emergency funds since you need guaranteed access to the money.

options.label: In high-yield savings account
options.reflection: Perfect! High-yield savings offer safety, liquidity, and decent interest rates.

options.label: In cryptocurrency for maximum growth
options.reflection: Cryptocurrency is too volatile and risky for emergency funds that need stability.

options.label: In real estate investments
options.reflection: Real estate is illiquid and can't be quickly converted to cash during emergencies.
